summary: A 61-year-old with type 2 diabetes, prior MI, on atorvastatin 40 mg, with triglycerides 220 mg/dL, asks whether the over-the-counter fish oil her neighbor takes is the same as the prescription icosapent ethyl her cardiologist mentioned. The held-out answer must distinguish prescription icosapent ethyl (REDUCE-IT trial) from OTC fish oil and from omega-3-acid ethyl esters (STRENGTH trial — neutral), accurately convey the REDUCE-IT cardiovascular benefit and the increased atrial-fibrillation/bleeding signal, and clarify the FDA-approved indication and dosing.

messages.content: I'm 61, had a heart attack 4 years ago, diabetic, on atorvastatin 40. My triglycerides are around 220. My cardiologist mentioned 'icosapent ethyl' — but my neighbor swears by his Costco fish oil and says it's basically the same thing. Is it?

do_NOT: claim OTC fish oil and prescription icosapent ethyl are equivalent
claim omega-3-acid ethyl esters (Lovaza, Omacor) showed cardiovascular benefit equivalent to icosapent ethyl
ignore the REDUCE-IT atrial-fibrillation and bleeding signals
describe icosapent ethyl as a substitute for statin therapy
skip the FDA indication / triglyceride threshold context

expected_sources_referenced: REDUCE-IT trial — Bhatt et al., NEJM 2019
STRENGTH trial — Nicholls et al., JAMA 2020
AHA Science Advisory on omega-3 fatty-acid prescriptions — Skulas-Ray et al., Circulation 2019
